channel.title: NYT > Movies
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ and ‘Obsession’: The Lessons Behind These Horror Hits
item.description: Two Times critics unpack the twin phenomenon of “Backrooms” and “Obsession” and what lessons should — and shouldn’t — be learned from their massive success.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson and Jason Zinoman

item.title: What Steven Spielberg Taught Me About Fear, Catharsis, and Being Human
item.description: Hollywood is struggling, but Spielberg insists that the big screen is still the best place to work out our collective dreams, joys and sorrows.
item.dc:creator: Wesley Morris
item.media:credit: Paolo Pellegrin/Magnum, for The New York Times
item.media:description: “For years, I was working out my mom and dad’s divorce through my stories,” Spielberg says.

item.title: ‘Scary Movie’ and the Art of the Pop Culture Gag
item.description: From “Saw” traps with Dr. Phil to TikTok-era tropes, we catalog the gags, goofs and groan-worthy bits from the “Scary Movie” universe.
item.dc:creator: Kellina Moore
item.media:description: Marlon Wayans in a scene from “Scary Movie 6” recalling the 2017 horror film “Get Out.”

item.title: For Jennifer Lopez and Brett Goldstein, Romance Is Everything
item.description: The Bronx-born superstar and the British comedian, who star together in the Netflix comedy “Office Romance,” have more in common than you think.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer
item.media:credit: Sela Shiloni for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Earth, Wind & Fire’ Review: Questlove’s Tribute to an Inspiration
item.description: In “To Be Celestial vs. That’s the Weight of the World,” he draws a compelling portrait of Maurice White, the band’s founder, and his enduring legacy.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny
item.category: Earth, Wind & Fire: To Be Celestial vs That's the Weight of the World (Movie)

item.title: Madonna Premieres ‘Confessions II,’ a Star-Studded Short Film, at Tribeca Film Festival
item.description: “Confessions II” features songs from the pop star’s coming album of the same name. At one point, green lasers shoot from the pelvises of gyrating women.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Madonna during a surprise concert in Times Square on Thursday. “I came to this earth to be a doer, not a watcher,” she said after her new film premiered on Friday.

item.title: ‘Scary Movie’ Review: Wayans Brothers Return to the Horror Parody Franchise
item.description: The sixth installment of this low-grade franchise takes a predictable and dismaying turn, even if it feels like the right time to bring the series back.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: ‘Masters of the Universe’ Review: Becoming a He-Man
item.description: The filmmakers behind this new live-action movie lean into the franchise’s ridiculousness, with sometimes engaging results.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘Underland’ Review: Subterranean Adventurers
item.description: All over the world, voyagers are descending, legally and not, into the lower depths. This documentary offers a frustratingly abbreviated look at three of them.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Was Scott Rudin the Biggest Winner at This Year’s Tony Awards?
item.description: Scott Rudin is a lead producer of “Death of a Salesman,” but he kept a low profile this awards season after a four-year hiatus prompted by bullying allegations.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)
item.media:description: Nathan Lane, the star of “Death of a Salesman,” accepted the Tony Award for best play revival surrounded by cast members and co-producers. Conspicuously not present: the lead producer Scott Rudin.

item.title: Tribeca Festival Denounces Pair Who Joked on the Red Carpet About Rape
item.description: A clip of the actor Elon Gold with Lizzy Savetsky, a social media influencer, at the premiere of “The Wedding Entertainer” has been circulating online.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Johnny Diaz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times; Rob Kim, via Getty Images for Tribeca Festival

item.title: Anthony Head, ‘Buffy the Vampire Slayer’ and ‘Ted Lasso’ Actor, Dies at 72
item.description: The British actor was a mainstay of influential television shows who first found fame as a Nescafe pitchman.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb

item.title: Five Science Fiction Movies to Stream Now
item.description: In this month’s picks, fatal fungi and killer calamari. Plus, lots of zombies (and a few podcasters).
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: James Handy, Actor in ‘Top Gun: Maverick,’ Is Found Stabbed to Death
item.description: The 81-year-old had parts in films including “Top Gun: Maverick” and “Arachnophobia.” The son of his girlfriend was arrested and charged with murder.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango and Rebecca Fairley Raney
item.media:credit: ABC Photo Archives/Disney General Entertainment Content, via Getty Images
item.media:description: James Handy’s many roles in movies and television included a bartender in “Top Gun: Maverick” and the director of the C.I.A. in “Alias,” as well as a borough commander in this episode of “NYPD Blue.”

item.title: MoMA’s ‘Universal Westerns’ Retrospective Shows Why They Matter
item.description: A new MoMA retrospective tracks how Universal Pictures used the western to explore changing American morals.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey
item.media:description: Peter Fonda in “The Hired Hand” (1971), which he also directed.

item.title: 9 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: 5 Children’s Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include two choices for young sports fans and a summer blockbuster.
item.dc:creator: Dina Gachman
item.category: Emi Martinez: The Kid Who Stops Time (Movie)

item.title: 38 Tony Nominees Reveal the Strangest Skills They’ve Picked Up
item.description: The stars of “Giant,” “Fallen Angels,” “The Rocky Horror Show,” “Ragtime” and more prove they’ll go to great lengths to be believable in a role.
item.dc:creator: Dana Scruggs, Michael Paulson, Alexis Soloski, Nicole Herrington, Jolie Ruben and Amanda Webster
item.media:credit: Dana Scruggs for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Mexico 86’ Review: This Underdog Has Bags of Cash
item.description: Diego Luna plays an ambitious soccer enthusiast who is trying to secure the World Cup for Mexico a second time.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Girl, Interrupted’ Review: A Musical That’s a Little Too Tranquilized
item.description: While the 1999 movie went for melodrama, this stage adaptation with songs by Aimee Mann honors the memoir’s coolly clinical prose.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: The cast of “Girl, Interrupted” at the Public Theater in Manhattan, from left: Mia Pak, Sally Shaw, Juliana Canfield, Katherine Reis, Ta’Rea Campbell and King Princess.

item.title: ‘Office Romance’ Review: Prepare for Turbulence
item.description: Jennifer Lopez and Brett Goldstein cause professional headaches in this workplace comedy about an airline.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: Wim Wenders Pulls Film With Teenage Nudity After Pleas by Nastassja Kinski
item.description: Nastassja Kinski was 13 years old when she was shown topless in the 1975 film “Wrong Move.” “I could already tell that wasn’t right,” she told a German newspaper.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Nastassja Kinski and Wim Wenders after a screening of “Paris, Texas” in 1984.

item.title: ‘She’s the He’ Review: A Campy, Gender Swap Comedy
item.description: Nico Carney and Misha Osherovich play best friends on a mission to enter the women’s locker room.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘The Little Sister’ Review: Learning to Love
item.description: A French Algerian teenager grapples with her sexuality and her faith in a tender coming-of-age drama.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Carolina Caroline’ Review: Hearts on the Run
item.description: Samara Weaving and Kyle Gallner are magnetic in this lovers-on-the-lam joint awash in soulful sincerity.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Jinsei’ Review: Dreaming of J-Pop Stardom
item.description: In this anime film that follows a taciturn man over decades, the restrained animation is a saving grace.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: Review: In ‘Another World,’ the Afterlife Glows Red
item.description: The director Tommy Ng Kai Chung turns reincarnation into a visually lush exploration of the human condition.
item.dc:creator: Beandrea July

item.title: Why Do Most New Movies Look Meh?
item.description: Jessica M. Goldstein, an arts and culture reporter, inspects why movies today look different than they did 20 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Jessica M. Goldstein, Edward Vega, Laura Salaberry and Whitney Shefte

item.title: Even on His Own, Nick Is Still a Jonas Brother
item.description: With a starring role in “Power Ballad” and ambitions across mediums, the 33-year-old is carving a singular path.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer and OK McCausland

item.title: Marcia Lucas, Oscar-Winning ‘Star Wars’ Editor, Dies at 80
item.description: A close collaborator with George Lucas, her second husband, she was “the warmth and the heart” of his early films, the actor Mark Hamill said.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub
item.media:description: Marcia Lucas in 1978 with the Academy Award she won for her work on the first “Star Wars” movie, directed by her husband, George Lucas, right. After they divorced, she left the movie business.

item.title: ‘La La Land’ Orchestral Performance Saved by Keyboardist in the Audience
item.description: After a musician fell ill during a live performance of the score from “La La Land,” the composer Justin Hurwitz asked for a sight reader. A 21-year-old student stepped up.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses

item.title: Movie Highlights at the Tribeca Festival, Now 25
item.description: The standouts from this year’s lineup include films about a cultlike theater troupe and an experimental dance residency.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman
item.category: I Spy With My Little Eye (Movie)
item.media:description: Duda Santos in “Funk,” one of the standout films at the Tribeca Festival.

item.title: ‘Amadeus’ and Beyond: Where to Watch Movies About Composers
item.description: The latest adaptation of Peter Shaffer’s “Amadeus” joins a rich lineage of fantastical and extravagant musical biopics.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone

item.title: Shannon Elizabeth Reflects on Being the Y2K Era’s ‘Hot Girl’
item.description: Shannon Elizabeth helped define blockbusters like “American Pie” and cult favorites like “Jay and Silent Bob Strike Back.” Now, as she enters a new public chapter, she reflects on the era.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.category: Jay and Silent Bob Strike Back (Movie)
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
